reasoning: Both models skipped the only file (PKGBUILD) due to a tooling bug that misclassified PKGBUILD as a binary file. Zero files were actually reviewed, so no security assessment was performed. The verdict cannot be determined from these reports. Notably, PKGBUILD is a plain-text shell script and should never be treated as binary — this is a false 'binary file' detection in the audit tool itself, not a property of the package.
disagreement_analysis: There is no real disagreement. Both models produced identical 'skipped' outcomes for the same reason: the tool's pre-audit step flagged PKGBUILD as a binary file and aborted before any model reasoning occurred. Both models consumed 0 tokens, confirming they never actually examined content.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed by any model — it was skipped during the initial pre-audit gate.
No .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts (if any) were enumerated or reviewed.
100% of files were skipped; effective coverage is zero.
false_positives: The 'Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD' classification is a false positive — PKGBUILD files are plain-text shell scripts and must always be treated as text.
false_negatives: Any actual risk in the PKGBUILD (download sources, install hooks, mime database update commands, permission changes) is entirely unassessed because the file was never read.
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — force text-mode review, inspect source/url, sha sums, prepare/build/package functions
Any .install scripts (post_install/post_upgrade) that may run update-mime-database or update-desktop-database
Confirm package only registers MIME types and does not fetch remote content
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic incorrectly classified PKGBUILD as binary, aborting the entire audit. PKGBUILD (and other known plain-text AUR files like .install, .desktop, .SRCINFO) should be whitelisted as text regardless of byte-content heuristics.
The pre-audit gate should fail-safe: if the primary PKGBUILD cannot be read, the run should be escalated/retried rather than silently returning 'skipped' with no coverage.
Add a guard that flags zero-file/zero-token audits as non-results requiring re-audit, rather than passing them through as completed reports.

Investigate why the binary heuristic triggered — likely an encoding issue (e.g., non-UTF-8 bytes, BOM, or CRLF) or a content sniff false positive; consider relaxing thresholds for files under a small size or with known shell shebang/keywords.
Bulk-review orchestration should detect when multiple models all skip the same file for the same tooling reason and surface it as an infrastructure bug, not a package verdict.
